title: Queues, Streams, Super Streams, Retention, and Replay · Inference Brief: Stream Retention Offset
summary: A high-throughput replay workload uses one quorum queue with a ten-million-message backlog, while another service selects streams but expects destructive consumption and ordinary queue client locality. Planned artifact: Add and verify the stream retention offset increment in queue-versus-stream workload decision and replay plan. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Define age and byte retention, segment behavior, start offset, consumer progress, replay authorization, and late-reader expectations.
Compare destructive queue consumption with retained stream offsets, replay, retention, protocol clients, and consumer responsibility.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and TypeScript Predict Stream Retention Offset · Theory Stream Retention Offset
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory messaging team handles case 1265 in a canary consumer reading old and new envelope versions. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to choose queue, stream, or super stream from replay, backlog, throughput, and order-key evidence; retain a screen-reader-readable delivery and ownership trace; then raise a broker resource alarm and stop new publisher admission. Browser code models pure messaging decisions only. amqplib, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, external effects, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Stream Retention Offset contract for the supplied data.
